items.title: Spam filter at deployment threshold
items.scenario: A logistic regression spam model outputs P(spam). Product sets threshold 0.7 (stricter than 0.5). On 10,000 holdout emails: TP=420, FP=80, TN=9,400, FN=100. Support reviews the matrix — 100 FN are missed spam (customer pain), 80 FP are ham marked spam (user annoyance).
items.explanation: One matrix at one threshold makes trade-offs visible. Raising the threshold would shrink FP but grow FN — the matrix quantifies that move before changing the cutoff.

items.title: Medical test confusion (disease positive = 1)
items.scenario: A diabetes screen on 5,000 patients (500 diabetic): at the clinic's chosen cutoff, TP=450, FN=50, TN=4,200, FP=300. Clinicians read TPR = 450/500 = 90% sensitivity and FPR = 300/4500 ≈ 6.7% before adjusting the referral threshold.
items.explanation: Clinical decisions fix a threshold first; the confusion matrix reports sensitivity (TPR) and false-alarm rate (FPR) on real populations — not abstract AUC alone.

items.title: Fraud model error analysis
items.scenario: After picking threshold from ROC (TPR ≥ 85%), analysts pull FP transactions (legitimate flagged as fraud) and FN transactions (fraud missed). The confusion matrix counts (TP=170, FP=40, TN=98,000, FN=30) drive a sprint to add features that reduce FN without doubling FP.
items.explanation: The matrix is the starting point for qualitative error analysis — which cell is hurting the business determines what to fix next.

items.title: Multi-class digit confusion (MNIST-style)
items.scenario: A 10×10 confusion matrix for digits 0–9 shows 980 on the diagonal but 12 examples of true '4' predicted as '9'. The team focuses relabeling and augmentation on that off-diagonal pair instead of chasing overall accuracy alone.
items.explanation: Multi-class matrices generalize binary TP/FP/TN/FN — off-diagonal mass reveals which class pairs the model confuses.
